question: Explain @State and @Binding in SwiftUI.
What is @StateObject vs @ObservedObject? When do you use each?
answer:
Both wrappers track an `ObservableObject` reference type and re-evaluate body when its `@Published` properties change. The difference is OWNERSHIP and LIFETIME:

• @StateObject — the view OWNS the object. SwiftUI creates it once and keeps it alive across body re-evaluations. Use when this view is responsible for instantiating the model.
• @ObservedObject — the view OBSERVES an object owned elsewhere. SwiftUI does NOT keep it alive; if you instantiate the object inline, it'll be re-created on every re-render and you'll lose state silently.

Rule of thumb: the view that creates the model uses @StateObject; downstream views receive it via @ObservedObject (or environment).

question: What is @EnvironmentObject? When do you reach for it?
What is the Observation framework (@Observable) introduced in iOS 17?
answer:
The Observation framework replaces the ObservableObject + @Published pattern with a more granular and ergonomic model.

• Mark a class `@Observable` (a macro) — every stored property automatically becomes observable; no @Published needed.
• Use a regular `@State var vm = MyVM()` to own it; pass it directly without @ObservedObject.
• Views only re-render for properties they actually read inside `body` — fine-grained tracking instead of "any @Published change triggers refresh".
• @Bindable creates a Binding to an Observable's property.

Result: less ceremony, fewer accidental re-renders, simpler mental model.

question: How does SwiftUI know when to re-render a view? Identity, lifetime, dependencies.
answer:
SwiftUI's render cycle has three concepts:

1) Identity — how SwiftUI distinguishes views across body re-evaluations. Two flavors: structural (position in the tree) and explicit (`.id(...)`, `Identifiable`). Same identity ⇒ same view; different identity ⇒ teardown + new lifetime.

2) Lifetime — span between view creation and removal. State (@State, @StateObject) is bound to lifetime; if identity changes, state resets.

3) Dependencies — what each view reads (state, environment, bindings). When a dependency changes, SwiftUI invalidates and re-runs `body` for the views that read it.

Gotcha: changing the structural position (e.g. wrapping in `if`) changes identity and resets state. Use `.id(stableValue)` to control identity explicitly.

question: How do you handle async data loading and tasks in SwiftUI views?
answer:
Two view modifiers tie SwiftUI to Swift Concurrency:

• `.task { await ... }` — runs an async task when the view appears; cancels automatically when the view disappears. Re-runs if you provide a `id:` value that changes.
• `.refreshable { await ... }` — wires up pull-to-refresh in List/ScrollView; the closure is async, the system shows a spinner until it finishes.

For model-owned tasks, use a `@StateObject`/`@Observable` view model with async methods. Bind UI to its loading/error state.
